meta.title: I Called Astronauts Inside SpaceX's Dragon Capsule In Polar Orbit! (FRAM2)
meta.description:
I got to talk to the FRAM2 astronauts while they were in polar orbit! This was the first ever crewed mission over the north and south poles. Stick around to the end to see my footage of them splashing down from a plane! 

--------------------------

Want to support what I do? Consider becoming a Patreon supporter for access to exclusive livestreams, our discord channel! - http://patreon.com/everydayastronaut

Or become a YouTube member for some bonus perks as well! - https://www.youtube.com/channel/UC6uKrU_WqJ1R2HMTY3LIx5Q/join

All music is original! Find it anywhere you listen to music! (Spotify, iTunes, Google Play, Amazon, etc)

transcript.text: Okay, all stations, this is core on
dragon to ground, but we are receiving
video from the capsule and we're
successfully getting that through. So,
at this time, I'd like to say that you
are go for the media
event. Dragon copy
[Music]
[Applause]
ignition and liftoff.
[Applause]
From 2 launched on March 31st, 2025 on a
SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket from Kennedy
Space Center's launch complex 39A. It
was the first ever crude mission to fly
over the north and south poles, so a
polar orbit. And I had the honor of
talking to them while they were in orbit
inside the SpaceX Crew Dragon capsule.
The mission was funded by entrepreneur
Chun Wang, who brought along Yanuka
Mickelson, Rabia Rogue, and Eric
Phillips, who are all Arctic explorers
that Chun met on an expedition to the
North Pole. What I love about this
mission is the candid nature in which
Chun shot and shared his perspectives
from his phone throughout the mission,
including closing of the hatch, on orbit
activities, and even the de-orbit burn.
Later in this interview, you'll see that
Chun actually starts shooting from his
phone and recording from his phone. So,
the quality actually gets a lot better,
and it's a really unique perspective on
a space to ground conversation that I've
never really seen before. Okay, let's
hear from 2. Good morning from crew of
From 2. Uh, how is everything going up
there? Have you guys uh recovered from
the day one? Sounds like uh some day one
acclamations. I hope you guys are doing
well. How are you doing?
Great. We are currently flying over uh
North Atlantic Ocean and soon we'll uh
fly over Greenland. And here we have
five guys here and uh I'm Tun. And uh
let me introduce uh Yanuka uh Rabia,
Eric, and also
Tyler. Amazing. It looks so incredible
up there. Uh what's what's surprised you
the most so far about your mission? I
mean, this is the first time humans have
ever flown over the poles. What's what's
been the biggest surprise so far?
Well, the biggest surprise that we think
we noticed is that we see the planet
that's mostly white because we get to
see the North Pole and the South Pole.
Yeah, we really do. I mean, this is not
a surprise that we would spend so much
time over the Arctic and Antar, but it's
it it's an absolute joy because we're at
the North Pole and at the South Pole
every 45 minutes we fly over a pole and
we see white 5 minutes leading up to the
pole and 5 minutes uh on the other side
of the pole and we do that twice every
orbit. Uh so we are seeing a lot of ice
and snow. We see cracks in the Arctic
Ocean. We see mountains in Antarctica.
There's so much to see here that is
white and beautiful. It's incredible,
right?
Yeah. And I would add that I think the
most surprising thing that took me most
back is uh we can see the day and night
shift over the poles and it is just so
stunning. I was not prepared for that.
I was expect to see some human
existence, human signs in Antarctica,
but unfortunately while flying too high
and I couldn't see anything uh just the
white uh snow um sea uh but no human
unfortunately.
Have you been able to uh see any auroras
yet? Have there been the correct
lighting conditions at all to see, you
know, uh, northern lights or I guess
southern lights or or anything like that
at all?
Absolutely. We have been very lucky and
filmed both the aurora astralis and the
aurora borealis and we actually seen
some interesting fragments and also some
new phenomenas that we were not aware
of. So we are really looking forward to
sharing those videos with you.
I also checked online there someone said
yesterday there used to be a uh level
two solar event. We didn't uh further
confirm that. Uh but one thing uh I
didn't anticipate is uh uh we when we
flying over the post uh we couldn't see
much aurora because uh uh down below
it's a dark but up there uh here it's uh
already have a sunrise. So uh we are
under uh under daytime below under uh in
the dark. So we couldn't see anything
below uh during that period.
That makes sense. I I mean, that's just
crazy to think that you're able to see
things that no human has ever seen
before. What's What's that actually feel
like? Is that does that reoccurring that
are you realizing in the middle of this
moment that you know, no human has seen
this before, ever? Is that like in the
back of your minds all the time? And is
it And I'll I'll piggyback on that. Is
it hard to sleep because you're also
probably wanting to like just look
outside so much?
I do think the polar orbit is the best
orbit human can fly. So I think any
follow uh follow up missions should fly
this orbit.
Yeah, there is no question that we are
seeing things that no humans have ever
seen with their eyes before and we are
just loving every moment of it. I mean,
I've skied four new routes up glacias
that had never traveled before and AC ac
across the plateau to the South Pole and
for the opportunity to to see well kind
of the beginning of those roots cuz high
up on the plateau it's now dark. We are
on the dark side of the the equinox. Um
so for me in particular um having seen a
lot of Antarctica that has never been
seen before. to see it from this view
and from this expanse, it's
mindboggling. And the Arctic Ocean too,
we are seeing expansive parts of the
Arctic Ocean that are that are new to
humans as well. And um you know to see
them on Google Earth and to see them on
uh on satellite images or so it it's
kind of different you know it's kind of
so special. That's all I can say.
And just within a few minutes you can
see the change of seasons such like we
just saw India and we flow over the
Himalayas into Tibet and then Mongolia
Gobi desert uh and Lake Boo and inter
Russia and the Arctic Ocean within just
20 minutes. It's the entire season
changed.
That is
incredible.
Has it been um as far as uh you know we
were talking about surprises earlier. Um
has it been difficult to acclimate to
you know four people inside such a small
spacecraft? Uh you know obviously you
have to be very comfortable with each
other and especially with the day one's
uh activities we'll call it. Has there
been any surprises there? Obviously, you
guys are pretty close together having
done a lot of training and things like
that, but um how's that been going as
far as actually um acclimating to being
in such a tight quarters together?
Yeah, I think right now we are the
closest that we would ever get. Um and
still we're comfortable. So, um I think
uh we've been very comfortable uh during
this mission. And I think what uh
strikes me the most is also that we're
in the spacecraft uh and you've got
Earth below and the stars above. And I
think we just have the feeling that you
know, humanity also belongs to the
stars. So we're exploring from a polar
orbit right now, but really the next
step step is to explore the stars.
Speaking of stars, are you actually able
to see even though it's daylight right
now? It looks like um if you look out
the correct window and you let your eyes
adjust, are you actually able to see uh
stars still?
Absolutely. When it is pitch black
outside and believe me, it is black like
a void, we see stars and and also
satellites. And because I'm a city boy,
it's probably my first time see the
Milky Way.
Wow, that is
awesome. Um, so I I assume obviously
we're going to get some absolutely
incredible images. I know that Yanuka's
filming a lot of stuff. I'm sure you're
all filming a lot of incredible images.
Uh, what's what's been a has there been
any challenges with with capturing video
and and photos and uh how has that been
going as far as the some of the film
projects?
So absolutely I mean I'm capturing
everything in 8K and bringing up the
latest of camera technology which is
giving me incredible images but also
presenting a few challenges because we
don't have gravity. I've noticed that
some of the high technology doesn't work
in microgravity. So you know it's been
going back to the basics of working
almost analog with digital technology.
And I know that you've been doing um
some some 3D work as well, uh 360 and
8K. Um but yeah, so some of the things
haven't been working quite as planned,
but is uh uh overall though, do you feel
like you're some of the experiences will
be uh able to work out for you guys?
Absolutely. Um, I mean I'm I feel like
I'm speaking to a fellow cinematographer
here and I feel like you would have
cinematographer envy if you were here
and saw the images that we are
capturing. Our goal is to make you feel
like you are here with us on the FRAM 2
mission and seeing the poles together
with us on this incredible uh
groundbreaking mission that is exploring
the last frontier of low earth orbit.
I absolutely cannot wait to see those
images. Um I'm curious, uh you guys must
have uh a way to be able to track uh
radiation. Has there been anything uh
different, you know, cuz you're flying
over the poles and that's kind of where
the the radiation, you know, the
ionizing radiation obviously gathers
over the poles. Um, has there been any
surprises there or or can you can you
tell or is there any other signs that
like, oh, we're flying in a a different
trajectory than most human space
flight's ever gone?
Yeah, let me answer the first part of
the question. Uh, for the radiation, um,
we are tracking our, uh, radiation with
personal dosimters and also a mission
dositter. Um so we are very interested
to see or very excited to share the
results after the mission. Um but also
we have um we are very happy to announce
that we had the first ever X-ray in
space taken today uh very successfully.
Um which is I know a tangent to
radiation but I think we're just so
excited to share that. Uh, so I'll just
put that out there and we can show you
what we and we and we can actually show
you, can we? What we
[Music]
And not only will you we show you the
images and also I've been paying
attention to the cameras because there's
8K. I was worried that the cosmic
radiation would ruin the sensors of the
cameras, which I have not. And I was
also curious if we would experience
those flashes, you know, that the Apollo
astronauts spoke about when they shut
their eyes at night and they were
falling asleep. They would see these
small flashes of light. I I think I've
seen one, but I'm not 100% sure. So, I
don't know how to confirm if that is
something that we have experienced or
not.
Wow. That is that is absolutely crazy.
Uh, now with the X-ray that that makes
me feel like um Oh, here comes the I
don't know if that's the machine there,
but um I Oh, there we go. Yeah, there's
And whose hand is that? Is that Is that
June's hand? I saw that posted on X and
that is about the coolest thing I have
ever seen.
You know, it's it's actually my hand
there. Um, but you know, if I hadn't
been fortun, I would never have had my
hand x-rayed in space and that being the
first human having
their first such a human being x-rayed
in space. So, huge thank you here to
Chin Wang.
That is fantastic. I have to say I've
been absolutely loving uh the updates uh
and especially just the the casual
nature, how candid uh we're seeing
spaceflight from you guys. I mean, we're
used to seeing some private missions
and, you know, we've seen some
incredible things from uh Inspiration 4
and the players crews, but there's still
another level yet of like just this is
just for humans and you just get feel so
connected. Um, I I've been absolutely
loving these updates and I really hope
you're able to to keep sharing uh your
experience from these perspectives, too,
because it's just so fascinating.
Yeah. Hey, Tim. I I I really hope that
we do come across um as being simply
ordinary astronauts, right?
Everyday astronauts. Yeah. Everyday
astronauts. Exactly. But ordinary too.
You know, we are ordinary citizens of
planet Earth and uh SpaceX trained us
up. It took the best part of a year or
so and here we are doing everything that
we have trained for. And boy are we
enjoying it too. You cannot wipe the
smiles from our faces.
Our goal is to bring space down to the
earth.
You truly are. You are living the
ultimate version of bringing space down
to earth for everyday people. I mean,
this is that mission to me. And it's
just been so fun. I mean, even the
perspectives like when you're doing c
the load out or the close out of the
capsule. Um, seeing that perspective
from literally firstperson view has been
incredible. Uh, having this view of you
guys in the in the Koopa is just
outstanding. Um, I am such a big fan of
this mission and I it's been so cool to
see how people react. Uh, let's let's
let's close out with everyone saying one
more final uh, you know, what what's one
more thing that you hope to be able to
do um, on this mission and what do you
hope to do when you splash down and go
home? Mission. And what do you hope to
do when you splash down and go home?
Let's start it.
Yeah. I really hope that um this mission
also carries all the enthusiasm and the
joy to individuals to people on the
ground and I hope that after this
mission maybe people are inspired to ask
themselves okay what is the biggest
thing I can dream of and if I'm not
following my dream what is stopping me.
[Music]
Yeah, I've I've said many times that
adventure is its own reward and that um
you know looking towards a horizon over
which we had no idea about like those
early from pioneers of Armenson and and
Frichoff we are and Frieov Nansen we are
kind of doing the same. We are flying
over horizons and seeing things that
people have never seen before. There are
opportunities to do this for other
people. Maybe not here in space, but
down on Earth. Lots of things to
do. And my mission is to make movies
that inspire the whole new generation of
people to become the best versions of
themselves and be inspired by planet
Earth and look after planet Earth
because she's fragile. And from up here,
she looks peaceful, but we know she's
fragile.
And dragon is a small spacecraft and uh
uh we try to push the boundary of the
limit of the uh spacecraft to uh what it
can do. So we fly pole to pole. uh given
like uh in a few years when Starship
become available I think there'll be a
lot of more interesting place we can go
and I can't believe like I think it's
probably the time to save money to you
know uh so that in few years we can
visit a lot of more uh interesting
destinations so now I'm trying to uh
visit every country in the world so the
next generation may start to explore the
solar system and see how How many people
can how many how many planets you can go
before you die? Right.
All finished.
So, is that confirmation from you, June,
that uh that this is just the start of
your uh spaceflight endeavors and that
you uh hope to be able to visit as many
planetary bodies as uh as humanly
possible in one lifetime?
As humanly possible in one lifetime.
say yes. Yes, of course.
You know, I I have to admit I I I don't
think I've ever talked to someone who's
uh whose life goal is to walk or or
visit planetary bodies. Uh and you're
you're making a heck of a splash here.
Now, now that you've trained on Dragon 2
and you talked about Starship, um do you
actually do you envision want, you know,
pursuing that as like is that already
the next goal for you? Are you already
while you're in space right now, are you
already considering trying to trying to
fly on Starship next? Considering trying
to trying to fly on Starship next.
uh that's uh that's a strong candidate
and uh for I must say it's still early
to confirm anything but that's uh
something uh uh I'm
considering that is absolutely
incredible. Well, I'm uh I've getting
close to the end of my time here. So,
yeah, give us one more one more goodbye
from everyone. I'm excited to see. Oh,
this is an incredible view. Look at
this. No way. That's so cool. Yeah, just
uh give us your your sign out here. This
is this is the perfect signoff
opportunity. Unfortunately, right as I
was saying goodbye, we did lose
communications. But that's actually not
the last time I saw the crew because
just a couple days later, I hopped in my
friend Jay's plane and we headed out to
the edge of the exclusion zone just off
the coast of California to see if we
could catch them splashing down. And
guess what? Just as they're about to
actually splash down, I managed to catch
them while they're under parachutes.
Now, don't kill me for the vertical
video. I I know it's really bad and we
were obviously quite far away because we
were outside of the exclusion zone, but
it was just crazy to see them under
parachutes and see them splash down
knowing that I just talked to them while
they were in space. What an awesome
mission. I can't wait to see all the
footage that they come back with. So,
huge thank you to Tune and the rest of
the crew of From 2 and SpaceX for
helping make this video possible. Well,
that's going to do it for me. I'm Tim
Dodd, the Everyday Astronaut, bringing
space down to Earth for everyday people.
